section: Chapter: Civilising the Native, Educating the Nation

questions.question: What types of educational institutions existed in India before British rule?
questions.explanation: Traditional Indian education was provided through institutions such as pathshalas, gurukuls, and madrassas where subjects like language, arithmetic, and religious texts were taught.

questions.question: What was the main aim of the British in introducing Western education in India?
questions.correct_answer: To spread Western knowledge and create a class of Indians who could assist in administration
questions.explanation: The British believed Western education would help create a group of educated Indians who could help them govern the country.

questions.question: Who were the Orientalists?
questions.correct_answer: British officials who supported education in Indian languages and classical texts
questions.explanation: Orientalists believed that Indian culture and knowledge should be preserved and promoted through education.

questions.question: Who were the Anglicists?
questions.correct_answer: British officials who supported English education and Western knowledge
questions.explanation: Anglicists believed that English should be the medium of instruction and Western knowledge should be taught in India.

questions.question: Who wrote the famous Minute on Indian Education in 1835?
questions.explanation: Macaulay strongly supported English education and argued that Western literature and science were superior to traditional Indian learning.

questions.question: What was the significance of Wood’s Despatch of 1854?
questions.correct_answer: It laid the foundation for a modern education system in India
questions.explanation: Wood’s Despatch recommended the establishment of universities, teacher training institutions, and expansion of primary education.

questions.question: Which universities were established in India in 1857?
questions.correct_answer: Universities of Calcutta, Bombay, and Madras
questions.explanation: These were the first modern universities established in India following the recommendations of Wood’s Despatch.

questions.question: Which Indian reformer supported the introduction of Western education?
questions.explanation: Raja Rammohan Roy believed that Western education and scientific knowledge would help modernize Indian society.

questions.question: How did colonial education contribute to the rise of nationalism in India?
questions.correct_answer: It introduced ideas of liberty, democracy, and rights
questions.explanation: Western education exposed Indians to political ideas that inspired them to question colonial rule.

questions.question: What was one major impact of colonial education on Indian society?
questions.correct_answer: The emergence of an educated middle class
questions.explanation: English education helped create a group of educated Indians who later played an important role in social reforms and the freedom movement.

notes.title: Traditional Education in India
notes.content: Before British rule, India had indigenous education systems like pathshalas, gurukuls, and madrassas supported by local communities.

notes.title: Anglicists vs Orientalists
notes.content: The debate between Anglicists and Orientalists focused on whether education should promote Western knowledge through English or traditional knowledge through Indian languages.

notes.title: Macaulay’s Education Policy
notes.content: Macaulay’s Minute of 1835 promoted English as the medium of instruction and encouraged the teaching of Western literature and science.

notes.title: Wood’s Despatch
notes.content: Wood’s Despatch of 1854 introduced a systematic education structure including universities, teacher training, and expanded schooling.

notes.title: Growth of Nationalism
notes.content: Western education exposed Indians to modern political ideas, helping the growth of nationalism and the freedom movement.

facts.fact: Traditional education in India existed long before British rule.
Macaulay’s Minute of 1835 promoted English education in India.
Wood’s Despatch of 1854 is often called the Magna Carta of English education in India.
Universities in Calcutta, Bombay, and Madras were established in 1857.
